rationale_for_baseline_tier: Acute exacerbation of a chronic headache with NEW bilateral peripheral (bitemporal) visual field loss (optic chiasm compression, threatening irreversible sight loss) plus clear positional unilateral rhinorrhoea (suspected spontaneous CSF leak, a direct portal for ascending bacterial meningitis). Both are patient-reportable, time-critical, vision- and life-threatening features that mandate urgent hospital imaging and neurosurgical/ENT review out-of-hours. The patient lives alone with new visual loss and cannot safely self-transport. The source clinician activated a 000 ambulance transfer. T5 (call 000, ambulance transfer) is the honest tier; acute vision loss lifts this above the T3 telehealth-uplift ceiling.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Clear watery positional discharge from the nose with acute neurological features (suspected CSF leak)
escalation_edges.time_bound_condition: Now — arrange 000 ambulance transfer to a tertiary ED for immediate MRI and neurosurgical/ENT assessment
escalation_edges.rationale: A CSF leak is a neurosurgical emergency with meningitis risk; imaging and specialist review cannot wait for in-hours services.
escalation_edges.communication_template: The clear fluid from your nose with these symptoms may be spinal fluid leaking, which needs urgent hospital scans. I'm arranging an ambulance now — keep your head up and don't blow your nose while you wait.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: New or worsening bilateral peripheral vision loss with acute severe headache change
escalation_edges.time_bound_condition: Now — same emergency transfer; do not wait for in-hours imaging
escalation_edges.rationale: Chiasmal compression threatens permanent visual loss and overlaps with pituitary apoplexy — urgent decompression assessment is time-critical.
escalation_edges.communication_template: Losing your side vision with this headache means pressure on the nerves behind your eyes. Getting to hospital urgently gives the best chance of saving your sight — the ambulance is on its way.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Fever, neck stiffness, persistent vomiting, or drowsiness/confusion developing before or during transfer
escalation_edges.time_bound_condition: Immediately — dial 000 again to upgrade to a priority emergency response
escalation_edges.rationale: These are meningitic features; with a suspected dural defect they signal ascending meningitis needing the highest-priority response.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If you get a fever, a stiff neck, keep vomiting, or feel drowsy or confused, call 000 again straight away and tell them it's got worse — this could be a serious infection around the brain.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Collapse, seizure, or sudden complete loss of vision
escalation_edges.time_bound_condition: Immediately — 000 priority emergency; stay on the line
escalation_edges.rationale: Catastrophic neurological deterioration requires immediate paramedic resuscitation and rapid transfer.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If you collapse, have a fit, or your vision goes completely, that's an immediate emergency — call 000 and stay on the line so they can guide you.

contextual_modifiers.patient_factor: Lives alone with no one present, and has new bilateral visual field loss
contextual_modifiers.effect_on_advice: Self-transport is unsafe (visual loss + severe headache + no escort); an ambulance (000) rather than a drive-to-ED is the correct pathway, and the patient should unlock the door for paramedics and stay seated/head-elevated while waiting.

contextual_modifiers.patient_factor: Middle-aged adult with a suspected chronic pituitary/sellar background (fatigue, cold intolerance, oligomenorrhoea, galactorrhoea)
contextual_modifiers.effect_on_advice: Endocrine work-up (pituitary hormones) is important but secondary to the acute emergency; it should be arranged through the hospital, not allowed to delay transfer.

baseline_safety_net_advice.default_tier_advice: Call 000 for an ambulance now and tell them you have a suspected spinal-fluid leak from the nose with loss of side vision and a severe new headache. While you wait: stay sitting or semi-upright with your head elevated, do not blow your nose, cough hard, bend forward, strain or lift, and just gently dab the nostril with a tissue. Do not drive yourself. Unlock the front door for the paramedics and stay where you can be seen.
baseline_safety_net_advice.after_hours_advice: 000 operates 24 hours — this is an out-of-hours emergency and must not wait for clinic or imaging hours.
baseline_safety_net_advice.documentation_for_gp: Hospital will provide a discharge summary with MRI findings, the CSF-leak assessment and any neurosurgical/ENT plan; arrange endocrinology follow-up for pituitary hormone assessment (prolactin, thyroid, cortisol, gonadotrophins).

critical_under_triage.failure_description: Treating the presentation as migraine, a routine chronic headache, or a benign cold/allergy and deferring to self-care or a routine GP appointment.
critical_under_triage.patient_harm_scenario: Irreversible visual loss from untreated chiasmal compression, or ascending bacterial meningitis from the unrecognised CSF leak — potentially fatal.

serious_under_triage.failure_description: Recognising urgency but arranging only same-day GP/urgent-care rather than emergency imaging and neurosurgical assessment, delaying MRI and specialist review while vision deteriorates and infection risk persists.

moderate_over_triage.failure_description: T5 is the correct tier here, so over-triage is not a meaningful risk; the dominant error mode is under-triage.

clinical_uncertainty_handling.key_uncertainty: Telehealth cannot confirm the nasal fluid is CSF, cannot test visual fields formally, and cannot image — the underlying diagnosis cannot be confirmed remotely.
clinical_uncertainty_handling.correct_response_to_uncertainty: Treat the pattern as a suspected CSF leak with chiasmal compression and escalate emergently for imaging and specialist review; conservatism (over-escalation) is correct when a vision- and life-threatening structural cause cannot be excluded.
clinical_uncertainty_handling.wrong_response_to_uncertainty: Using the inability to confirm as a reason to reassure, to label it migraine/allergy, or to defer to a routine appointment.
